sourceQuestion:
Consider the two program segments below:
a.  for i := 1 to f(x) by 1 do
      S
    end
b.  i := 1;
    while i <= f(x) do
      S
      i := i + 1
    end

Under what conditions are these two programs equivalent? Treat S as any sequence of statements and f as a function.

A. They are always equivalent.
B. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x.
C. They are equivalent only if f(x) >= 1.
D. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x and f(x) >= 1.
sourceAnswer: B. They are equivalent only if S does not modify x.

    end
b.  i := 1;

    end

options.text: They are equivalent only if S does not modify x.

They are equivalent only if S does not modify x and f(x) >= 1.

sourceAnswer: A. n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m  and  rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1

options.text: n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m and rev = d_i d_(i-1) ... d_1
n = d_(i+1) d_(i+2) ... d_m and rev = d_1 d_2 ... d_i
n = d_1 d_2 ... d_(m-i) and rev = d_(m-i+1) ... d_m
n = d_1 d_2 ... d_(m-i) and rev = d_m d_(m-1) ... d_(m-i+1)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following pseudo code, where x and y are positive integers.

begin
  q := 0
  r := x
  while r >= y do
  begin
    r := r - y
    q := q + 1
  end
end

The post condition that needs to be satisfied after the program terminates is

A. {r = qy + r ∧ r < y}
B. {x = qy + r ∧ 0 <= r ∧ r < y}
C. {x = qy + r ∧ 0 < r ∧ r <= y}
D. {q + 1 < r - y / y > 0}
sourceAnswer: B. {x = qy + r ∧ 0 <= r ∧ r < y}

parsed.stem:
Consider the following pseudo code, where x and y are positive integers.

begin
  q := 0
  r := x
  while r >= y do
  begin
    r := r - y
    q := q + 1
  end
end

The post condition that needs to be satisfied after the program terminates is

sourceAnswer: B. The program prints 10 as output

options.text: The program gets stuck in an infinite loop

sourceAnswer: C. The program will terminate with 4321 as output

options.text: The program will terminate with no output
The program will terminate with 4321 as output
The program will terminate with 1234 as output

sourceQuestion:
In which of the following cases is it possible to obtain different results for call-by-reference and call-by-name parameter passing methods?

A. Passing a constant value as a parameter
B. Passing the address of an array as a parameter
C. Passing an array element as a parameter
D. Passing an array as a parameter
sourceAnswer: C. Passing an array element as a parameter

parsed.stem: In which of the following cases is it possible to obtain different results for call-by-reference and call-by-name parameter passing methods?

options.text: Passing a constant value as a parameter
Passing the address of an array as a parameter
Passing an array element as a parameter
Passing an array as a parameter

sourceAnswer: D. a = 6, b = 3 (first call) then a = 15, b = 12 (second call)

sourceQuestion:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: m = malloc(5); m = NULL;
   1: using dangling pointers
Y: free(a); a -> value = 5;
   2: using uninitialized pointers
Z: char *p; *p = 'a';
   3: lost memory

A. X-1, Y-3, Z-2
B. X-2, Y-1, Z-3
C. X-3, Y-2, Z-1
D. X-3, Y-1, Z-2

Assume the following C variable declaration:

int *A[10], B[10][10];

Of the following expressions:
I.   A[2]
II.  *A[2][3]
III. B[1]
IV.  B[2][3]

which will not give compile-time errors if used as left hand sides of assignment statements in a C program?

A. I, II, and IV only
B. II, III, and IV only
C. II and IV only
D. IV only
sourceAnswer: A. I, II, and IV only

sourceQuestion:
What does the following C-statement declare?

int (*f) (int *);

A. A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
B. A function that takes an integer as argument and returns an integer pointer
C. A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
D. A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns a function pointer
sourceAnswer: C. A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer

parsed.stem:

int (*f) (int *);

options.text: A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
A function that takes an integer as argument and returns an integer pointer
A pointer to a function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns an integer
A function that takes an integer pointer as argument and returns a function pointer
